data.title: Fortinet: SASE Subscription Fuels Margins
data.description:
Unified Platform Drives Subscription Mix Shift

We initiate coverage on Fortinet, Inc. (NASDAQ:FTNT) at Strong Buy with $148 PT. Fortinet provides integrated and automated cybersecurity across firewall, cloud, and endpoint. While consensus expects moderating firewall growth, we see an inflecting, margin-led mix shift into subscription run-rate fueled by unified platform architectures (think platformization of ServiceNow and Palo Alto with balance-sheet discipline and margin profile).

We forecast unified SASE and SecOps ARR compounding at a 24% CAGR through FY26E, yet most consensus models assume only mid-20% growth and flat share counts. In our model, that high-teens subscription acceleration drives incremental operating leverage worth ~$0.04 of EPS annually. On top of that, we layer in an annual repurchase of ~7mn shares (remaining authorization $1.6bn), which further boosts EPS by ~$0.02 per year. The combination of faster‐than‐modeled ARR growth and overlooked buyback tailwinds delivers a structural $0.06 per share of "hidden" earnings power that Street forecasts have yet to capture.

A 50× FY26E P/E is demonstrable double-digit earnings plus a margin of safety, given multiples remain meaningfully below history despite capital returns and $1.9 bn of FCF per year. While a capex pause or tariffs could create inflections, modeled upside is open and risk is quantified. Price to intrinsic value sets one of few asymmetric risk/reward in software as we enter what should be a multi-year product cycle.

AI Platform Growth Nexus

FTNT represents a rare inflection in AI platform expansion and can compound recurring revenues and competitive advantage for years. The market has yet to recognize that FTNT's secure networking franchise (>50% share of global firewall deployments) is rapidly becoming a distribution vector for deep integration of proprietary generative AI across the Security Fabric, a network-embedded platform with machine learning, GenAI models and automated response engines natively operating across >40 functions and all layers of the network, endpoint and cloud stacks to enable containment and remediation of threats in seconds (industry average today is days), while creating a scalable cost base (visible in expanding service GMs at 78.5% and overall co-level OM at 34% in the latest quarter).

Unified SASE ARR grew 26% y/y to $1.15bn, Security Ops ARR grew 30% year over year to $434mn and >90% of the sales force is now certified to monetize the AI-powered suite, driving >100% billing growth in upsell solutions such as FortiSASE and FortiAI. We believe platformization through AI and an underlying OS built for convergence creates a unique flywheel between customers and the rich telemetry on the Security Fabric, which can be used to improve ML and accelerate future upgrades. This flywheel underpins FTNT's DD+ guide for service revenue growth (even as hardware cyclicality subsides) and supports EPS growth well ahead of consensus through 2026. We view the AI expansion as the underappreciated driver of FTNT's shift from security appliance vendor to enterprise platform, with further margin leverage to come.

Recurring Revenue Shield

FTNT is entering a structurally advantaged era of margin resilience and recurring revenue durability that should enable it to outperform sector peers as security trends evolve. The service revenue mix, now the majority of the top line, grew 20% in 2024 and surpassed $4.8bn, catapulting its profit engine beyond hardware. The subscription backlog ramp reflects rigorous execution in unifying SASE, SecOps and managed services across more than 800,000 accounts globally, with Europe, the Middle East and Africa leading all regions in both absolute and relative terms.

In 2024, growth in security subscription and support services more than offset hardware volatility, driving gross profit up 18 percent and operating income up 45 percent as operating expense leverage compounded. Deferred revenue, a leading indicator of backlog and future recurring streams, stood at more than $5.7 billion entering the calendar year 2025 estimate, a near 20 percent year-over-year increase. Billings attributable to unified SASE and SecOps now represent 35 percent of the total, further reinforcing forward revenue durability.

We expect these metrics to deliver at least a 19 percent earnings per share compound annual growth rate through the fiscal year 2027 estimate, as high-margin recurring service revenue shields hardware cyclicality and the fund's investments in cloud security and artificial intelligence-powered expansion. The shift to a recurring software and subscription core provides a rare valuation anchor in cybersecurity, underwriting asymmetric upside even if macro volatility persists.

Rationale for street-Beating EPS

Our FY25E / FY26E EPS projections of $2.55 / $2.95 imply 12.8% / 15.7% y/y growth versus the consensus of $2.47 / $2.77. These above-street views reflect a faster mix shift toward high-margin subscription-led revenues as the firewall-refresh super-cycle takes hold in 2H25 and Unified SASE / AI-driven SecOps ARR expands at a 24% CAGR through FY26.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $6.76B 33 $7.60B 37
EBITDA $1.70B 33 $1.91B 37
Net Income $1.69B 33 $2.41B 37
EPS $2.47 26 $2.77 27
SG&A Expenses $2.87B 33 $3.23B 37

We also assume repurchases of ~7mn shares annually, delivering a $0.06 to annual diluted EPS tailwind that consensus overlooks. Infosec management has proven its precision forecasting, delivering 5-10% EPS upside to guidance and averaging 18% above street consensus in recent quarters. Management's margin guidance of 31.5-33.5% for FY25E provides a floor as software mix increases, against which we model 32.8-33.5%. Key downside risks include tariff expansion, enterprise spending delays and competitive pricing responses, which, we believe, are factored into our 70% confidence score and scenario analysis. Overall, our conviction in above-street EPS stems from embedded operating leverage, strong product cycles and the factors above.

Valuation

Our $148 price target is based on a 50.0 times fiscal year 2026 estimated price to earnings ratio on our above-consensus $2.95 earnings per share, offering 44 percent upside and a Strong Buy rating. Our target multiple captures Fortinet's persistent 15.7 percent earnings per share compound annual growth rate and accelerating subscription revenue growth, leading 34 percent adjusted operating margins and consistent double-digit earnings per share beats.

The multiple sits below the historical median price to earnings ratio of 54.3 times and well below the peer median price to earnings ratio of 132.0 times (20th percentile versus 77th percentile), balancing Fortinet's dominant firewall position, secure access service edge-led expansion, and recurring $1.9bn free cash flow in fiscal year 2024 with allowances for insider selling and macro uncertainty. Fortinet's periods of trading near our target multiple have historically generated 64 percent in the forward twelve months, suggesting strong risk/reward. Overall, our valuation considers fundamentals and a margin of safety, reflecting the firm's inflection into a subscription-led model and leaving ample room for multiple expansion should the next product cycle arrive.

Risks to Our Strong Buy Thesis

While we assign high confidence to above-consensus EPS momentum from a subscription-led transformation and commanding firewall franchise, the thesis/valuation framework faces material risks. Most notably, escalations in U.S.-China/APAC tariffs could weigh on hardware sourcing and gross margin more than modeled (95% of FTNT manufacturing is in Taiwan; prolonged trade frictions or sanctions, especially amid escalating geopolitical tensions, could skew hardware production costs, inhibit supply and threaten modeled margin resilience and cash generation).

FTNT's moat also hinges on continued innovation ahead of a fast-evolving product taxonomy; if platform velocity stalls, the premium for integrated SASE/AI-driven SecOps could shrink materially if competitors such as PANW, CRWD and security-focused startups leapfrog its unified approach, jeopardizing FTNT's modeled ARR growth and multiple expansion. Lastly, investors should watch for macro-driven IT spend pauses broadening and firewall-refresh cycles extending beyond 2H25, as both would delay the subscription mix shift and recurring revenue compounding central to our EPS/valuation thesis.

Conclusion

We see Fortinet as one of the few asymmetric risk/reward stories in cyber, rooted in visible inflection to high-margin, subscription-led ARR growth and disciplined net earnings execution. With accelerating unified SASE and AI expansion, a sticky firewall franchise, and a below-historical and peer group multiple, we see material upside well ahead of consensus. Bottom line: We believe it's time to lean into the product cycle/compounding Op Leverage story-investors should treat short-term macro/tariff volatility as opportunities in a durable, margin-protected multi-yr growth story.

data.title: JOF: The Wide Discount To NAV Looks Set To Reduce
data.description:
Introduction

The $300m-sized Japan Small Capitalization Fund (NYSE:JOF), backed by Nomura Asset Management, has proven to be a very rewarding CEF [close-end fund] for its investors in the first half of 2025. In a year when the US markets (as represented by SPY), have only generated total returns of less than 7%, and global markets (as represented by VT) have just about managed double-digit returns, JOF, which focuses on close to 100 Japanese small-cap stocks, has outperformed these pockets by 2.5-4x.

If you’re swayed by this impressive performance, and are tempted to pursue a position in the same, here are some of the major subplots to consider.

Japanese Industrials Look Relatively Healthy, But Tariff Risk Looms

If JOF is to continue to flourish, we suspect, the industrial component of its portfolio will have to be in a good place. We say this because, Japanese industrial stocks dominate JOF, accounting for one-fourth of the total portfolio.

All in all, we feel that Japanese industrials are staring at a mixed outlook. Let’s start with what’s good. Firstly, we’re encouraged to see that the forward-looking manufacturing PMI recently crossed the expansionary barrier (readings over 50), for the first time in 13 months! The headline PMI number was also backed by improvements in output and employment.

Business sentiment for small Japanese manufacturers also appears to be trending in positive territory for now, after months of negative readings.

However, the clouds of tariffs are something that could jeopardize the relatively sanguine status quo. The latest report suggest that Japan is no close to agreeing a deal, and has, in fact, angered Trump to such an extent that he is now prepared to impose tariffs of 30-35% on Japanese exports, well above the 24% tariff that was initially announced in early April. This would come as a hammer blow to Japan, as the US is its largest export market.

Wide NAV to Discount on JOF, Looks Set To Narrow

As you can see from the image below, one of the long-standing drawbacks of JOF is that it has been trading at a steep discount to NAV for much of the last 15–20 years.

What’s worse is that as time has progressed, that discount has only widened, with the discount for the most recent year (as of March 2025) working out to over 18.4%, which is roughly 250bps more than what’s been seen over a 5-year time frame.

Fortunately, the board of JOF haven’t been viewing it with rose-tinted glasses, and last month, stated their intention to resort to a couple of initiatives that could help ameliorate the wide discount differential.

Firstly, they are going to follow a “level distribution plan” that will see monthly distributions equating to 10% of the NAV (as of May 31, 2025) on an annualized basis. This is quite a marked step-up as last year, the annualized distribution rate on the NAV was only a little over 3%.

For the next three months, they’ve already announced impressive distributions, equating to $0.0887 per share. For context, historically JOF has only distributed annually, and last year’s distribution worked out to only $0.3118.

By pivoting to the monthly distribution calendar, JOF is hoping to stoke up more interest from an untapped bracket of investors who normally would only stick to products with monthly distribution characteristics. With additional interest from this new cohort, there’s hope that the NAV discount could narrow.

In addition to the Level Distribution Plan, JOF could also launch a tender offer to buyback 10% of the outstanding shares, so long as the average daily discount to NAV, is wider than 9% from July 1st 2025 to March 31st 2026. As noted earlier, over the past year, the discount to NAV has averaged around 18%, so clearly there’s a lot of elbow room that they will have.

JOF’s Valuations Are Very Cheap

Besides these favorable developments, JOF is also likely to appeal to value conscious investors as its holdings are very cheaply priced, and the earnings runway that they offer are also not too dissimilar to what the holdings from the large-cap oriented abrdn Japan Equity Fund (JEQ) offer.

As per Morningstar data, JOF’s holdings can be picked up at a forward P/E of less than 8x, which works out to a 40% discount over JEQ. Yet, JEQ’s holdings are poised to deliver superior long-term earnings growth of only around 2%.

As an aside, investors may also want to be aware that even though JOF looks like it only pursues small-caps on paper (and this is also reflected in the average market-cap of its holdings that equates to $730m), the reality is that it also does offer some ownership to mid-caps which account for around a third of the total portfolio.

Closing Thoughts - Good Momentum, But Close To A Key Pivot Point

The fact that JOF is in the midst of bullish conditions can be gleaned from its daily chart, where the CEF is now trading well above its three key moving averages of 50, 100 and 200.

However before investors get carried away and join the bandwagon, they may also want to take note of developments on the larger time frame weekly chart.

What we can see is that after breaking out from an ascending channel (marked by the two dotted red lines), JEF has now reached a zone which has previously served as a pivot area. This could prompt a slowdown in bullish momentum, or potentially even a reversal in the price. Also note that the RSI indicator in the bottom half of the chart points to overbought conditions. The last couple of instances, when the RSI was at this juncture, CEF witnessed a price contraction.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Signs Of Activity Rebound
data.description:
Investment overview

NXP's (NASDAQ:NXPI) the largest vertical is the automotive segment, where it sells microcontrollers, software solutions for internal connectivity, and radars. NXP dominates the 77GHz radar transceiver market, with key applications in autonomous driving, alongside Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY). With the competition increasing between Google (GOOG)'s Waymo and Tesla (TSLA) in the US for the robotaxi race, NXP could be ideally positioned to benefit from such growth trends. Also, Chinese car manufacturers (led by BYD) are aggressively investing in ADAS features.

On top of that, it has a small exposure to EV via its solid positioning in battery management systems (where it competes with Analog Devices (ADI) and Texas Instruments (TXN). However, it has no exposure to power semiconductors.

The second segment is related to Industrial applications, where NXP owns a comprehensive portfolio of solutions for buildings, factories, trains, and tractors. In factories, semiconductors and software are enabling advanced automation and are a key to site productivity.

Then, the mobile unit handles solutions for device security (NFC wallet) and holds the largest market share, surpassing Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Growth could stem from the need for more security among connected devices. Finally, the firm operates in the communication infrastructure sector and dominates in High-Performance Radio Frequency power amplifiers used for 5G base stations. Unfortunately, the 5G industry CAPEX has disappointed over the recent years. It is unknown when this segment will bottom out.

NXP aims to grow its revenues by a CAGR of 8% in the coming years, driven by one quarter of its sales advancing at double-digit rates, thanks to positive penetration of advanced autonomous features within cars and AI applications. The legacy and core business (mobile, mobile infrastructure, and electronics non-correlated to ADAS in cars) are, however, expected to grow at a slow pace (3%) and underperform the global semiconductor market.

NXP holds solid market shares in microcontrollers MCUs and sensors. Overall, it is the second-biggest chip provider for the automotive industry.

In the next section, we will discuss recent quarterly results and highlight some notable advancements mentioned during the conference calls.

Recent quarterly trends

The management has recently stated, during the Q1 FY25 results, that forward revenues are expected to grow slightly sequentially. This is good news, as it stops the decline pattern seen in the graph below.

In addition, bullish comments indicated that we could be facing an activity rebound: “we are seeing some positive trends. These trends include improving distribution customer backlog levels as well as stabilized order signals from our direct customers. Additionally, we are experiencing an increase in short cycle orders as well as some spot product shortages leading to customer escalations. Taken together, these trends have historically been indicative of the early innings of improving cycle dynamics. »

Other peers, such as ON Semiconductor, have also recently indicated signs of activity rebound.

During the 53rd JPMorgan Tech conference call, the CEO transition was well explained. The current CEO Kurt Sievers insisted that the decision to leave was their own, and that they would go into early retirement after having well served the company. Kurt Sievers also introduced Rafael Sotomayor, who will take the CEO job by October 2025. He began his career at Intel, followed by a stint at Broadcom, where he developed a franchise in connectivity, then joined NXP in 2014 and managed the industrial and mobile business (close to half of the company in terms of revenues). Additionally, he co-developed the overall strategy, ensuring that the 2030 goal of doubling EPS through growth rebound and expanding the gross margin to 60% remains in place.

Also, the CEO reiterated its calls for a potential revenue bottom: « We think it's a sign of a cycle recovery », seeing better trends in terms of channel inventory and the level of customer backlogs. Another interesting part was related to the importance of China in NXP operations : « they are faster and they have to lead now in the transformation into SDVs. So, software defined vehicles, EVs, but also in industrial electronics, when it's about automation and robotics in factories, China is leading in the world. So, if we have now a priority call, where would we spend $1 R&D for a Western automotive company or for a Chinese one, we spend it for China. So that's the strategic ».

Despite China's willingness to increase its market share in the semiconductor market, most advanced electronics are likely to continue being produced by Western firms, including NXP. It is open to establishing both R&D and manufacturing sites in mainland China to serve its customers better, and notes that revenue directly coming from the Chinese market represents approximately 18% of its total sales.

Finally, NXP has recently been quite active in terms of M&A activity, having acquired three firms (Aviva, TTTech, and Kinara) for $1.1 billion to reinforce its high-tech capabilities, empowering its Software-Defined Vehicle CoreRide platform.

Let's examine the company's financials. Q1 FY25 Free Cash Flow was $427m, at a quarterly record low since 2024, but this could eventually be recognized as an activity bottom, given management commentaries. On the balance sheet, NXP recorded $4 billion in gross cash, $1.5 billion in short-term debt, and $10 billion in longer-term debt, well spread across maturities, as shown below. ND/EBITDA stood at 1.6X, which is reasonable and should allow buybacks and dividend payments to continue going forward, reaching a cumulative $2.4 billion in FY24 (4% of the market capitalization).

What valuation can we expect?

The main hypotheses used are the following:

  • Revenues are expected to rebound gradually starting in FY26, as distribution channels normalize, allowing under-shipping to end and growth to resume in the medium term, with an estimated 8% CAGR in line with management's end-market projections.
  • Gross margin expansion toward 60%, in line with management expectations (higher factory utilization, better product mix).
  • A FCF/revenues (cash-conversion) reaching 30% by the end of the decade, like most of the top-tier semiconductor companies.
  • By 2030, doubling Free Cash Flows, as the management sees X2 on EPS.

Using a WACC of 9% and a long-term growth rate of +3%, I calculate a DCF target price of USD 255 per share, indicating further upside potential

Risks

NXP faces several risk factors. First, competition is intense in the semiconductor industry, with several companies competing, including ON Semiconductor (ON), Infineon, STMicroelectronics (STM), Renesas (OTCPK:RNECF), Microchip (MCHP), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Analog Devices (ADI). Fortunately, we have seen that NXP holds solid market shares in its end markets. Continued investment and innovation are therefore key to maintaining its leadership. On top of that, Chinese semiconductor firms are becoming more competitive in the field of microcontrollers as they aim to reduce their dependence on Western companies. New entrants to the market could add pricing pressure to this mature market.

Then, cyclical risks could cause the short-term Price Earnings ratio to compress during a phase of economic slowdown or trade friction. We can see the impact of such events in the graph below.

Finally, a significant part of NXP's valuation stems from the growth expectations of its ADAS portfolio. NXP must deliver on that side or face low single-digit topline growth, which would negatively affect its valuation multiple.

Conclusion

NXP is ideally positioned in the semiconductor sector to serve the fast-growing ADAS segment, a key enabler to autonomous driving. The recent negative economic cycle and fears about trade tariffs have weakened financials and technicals. Good news could, however, be on the horizon as management indicates signs of activity rebound and inventory channel normalization going forward. With still upside potential, as noted in a DCF model, I rate the stock as a BUY.

data.title: HighPeak Energy Attempts To Refinance Its Debt With New Senior Notes
data.description:
HighPeak Energy (NASDAQ:HPK) is attempting to refinance its debt with a combination of senior notes and a new revolving credit facility. I had previously noted that dealing with its term loan maturity was a major priority, and HighPeak is now trying to address that.

HighPeak has reached an agreement on a new revolving credit facility, although that agreement is dependent on it being able to issue its proposed senior notes. There is uncertainty whether HighPeak can achieve acceptable pricing on those new notes. It has been several days since HighPeak announced its proposed note offering, and I've found that companies typically report the pricing on their proposed note offerings within a day or two of the initial announcement.

Thus the delay may indicate that HighPeak will need to look at alternative financing arrangements, similar to what happened in mid-2023.

I estimate HighPeak's value at $10.50 per share now, although that would be reduced to $10 per share in a scenario where it needs to issue equity to help reduce its net debt.

Past Refinancing History

In June 2023, HighPeak announced its intent to offer $575 million in senior notes due 2028 to pay off its $475 million in senior notes due 2024 (and also pay down a bit of credit facility debt).

It appears that HighPeak wasn't able to achieve acceptable pricing on those proposed new senior notes, so it ended up taking a different path.

HighPeak provided a financing update several weeks later, where it said that it was evaluating multiple prospective financing arrangements. It then issued 14.835 million shares at $10.50 per share for approximately $151.2 million in net proceeds, and a couple of months later closed on a $1.2 billion term loan.

Current Refinancing Efforts

HighPeak is now attempting to issue $725 million in senior notes due 2030 to help pay off (with the help of a new credit facility) its term loan debt, which was $1.05 billion at the end of Q1 2025. HighPeak also had $52 million in cash at the end of Q1 2025.

It has been several days since HighPeak made that first announcement. I've usually seen companies announce pricing for their note offerings the following day after the initial announcement. If HighPeak doesn't announce the note pricing by July 7, I believe that would be a strong indication that HighPeak is having challenges finding acceptable terms for its proposed note offering.

In any case, the interest on the new notes is likely to be in the double-digits even if HighPeak succeeds in issuing those new notes.

HighPeak also expects to enter into a new revolving credit facility agreement upon closing its notes offering. This new credit facility is expected to start off with a borrowing base and initial aggregate commitments of $720 million. It will mature in four years and has an interest rate (for SOFR loans) of SOFR +2.5% to +3.5%. That would be a significant improvement on the SOFR + 7.5% interest rate on HighPeak's current term loan.

Thus, even if the new senior notes had a 12% interest rate, HighPeak could still save around $15 million in annual interest costs.

Additional Hedges

HighPeak has added some more oil collars from Q4 2025 onward. These collars have a floor near $60 and a ceiling near $70 and may cover around 20% of HighPeak's oil production in 2026 (while covering closer to 40% of its oil production in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026).

At current strip prices, these new collars have neutral value, but they do provide a bit of downside protection should oil prices fall a few dollars compared to current strip. The WTI oil strip for 2026 is a bit over $62 right now.

Notes On Valuation

Due to there being some near-term improvement in oil prices since the last time I looked at HighPeak, I now estimate its value at approximately $10.50 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. This is a $0.50 increase from my last look at HighPeak. This assumes that HighPeak is able to issue those proposed new notes.

A share price of $10.50 would translate into an enterprise value of approximately $2.2 billion, based on HighPeak's projected year-end 2025 net debt of $0.88 billion.

HighPeak disclosed that it had proved developed reserves (at the end of 2024) with a PV-10 of $1.848 billion and proved reserves with a PB-10 of $2.486 billion at early June strip prices.

HighPeak's lower capex budget in 2025 may not result in full proved developed reserve replacement (particularly for oil). However, my long-term oil price (for 2027 onward) is higher than strip. At current strip (and then $70 WTI oil from 2027 onward) along with modestly lower proved developed oil reserves, HighPeak's proved developed PV-10 may be a bit over $1.9 billion.

As noted before, HighPeak may have challenges issuing $725 million in new senior notes due 2030. It may first need to issue shares to help reduce its new debt. In a scenario where it issues 25 million shares at around $8 per share (reducing its net debt by nearly $200 million net of offering costs), HighPeak's value would be reduced to approximately $10 per share instead.

Conclusion

HighPeak Energy is attempting to refinance its term loan due September 2026 with a combination of a new credit facility and new senior notes due 2030. There appears to be a decent chance that it is unable to achieve acceptable pricing for the notes without further reducing its net debt though.

HighPeak's proved developed reserves have a PV-10 (at early June strip) that is roughly double its current net debt. Raising $200 million via an equity offering would boost that coverage to around 2.6x.

Factoring in that potential equity offering, I estimate HighPeak's value at approximately $10 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil. HighPeak's estimated value would be slightly higher at $10.50 per share if it can avoid that dilution.

data.title: Granite Construction Offers Compelling Upside Thanks To Growth Plans
data.description:
One company that continues to impress me is Granite Construction (NYSE:GVA). In case you aren't familiar with the business, it operates in the construction industry. Specifically, it offers infrastructure solutions for public and private clients. And for these clients, it offers up site preparation, mining, and infrastructure services associated with a wide array of industries. Its work touches on streets, highways, mass transit facilities, airports, bridges, dams, railways, energy, and even residential development. While this might not seem like all that exciting a space to play in, I find it to be riveting. And honestly, since I have been bullish about the company, things have gone really well.

You see, back in April of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. I found myself impressed with its continued growth and its cheap valuation. At the end of the day, that call has proven to be successful. While the S&P 500 is up 18% since then, shares of Granite Construction have risen 20.9%. But honestly, that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in March 2023, the stock has risen by 130.3%. That's well over twice the 50.2% rise that the broader market experienced. The most recent fundamental data we have shows an admittedly mixed, but mostly positive, picture. On the whole, the stock does look cheap enough, both on an absolute basis and relative to similar firms, to maintain a solid ‘buy’ rating at this time.

Not Ready For A Downgrade… Yet

As of this writing, the only new data that we have out for Granite Construction covers the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Because of that, this will be our primary focus. During that time, management demonstrated continued growth for the business. Revenue came in at $699.5 million. That's 4% higher than the $672.3 million the business reported one year earlier. This growth in sales was driven by strength across both of its segments.

The largest of these, by far, is the Construction segment, which focuses on construction and rehabilitation for roads, pavement preservation, and really any other activity that I already covered at the start of this article. This growth for the segment came in spite of the fact that public revenue actually contracted from $419.8 million to $395.9 million. The rise in sales, then, came from the private side, with revenue expanding from $175.4 million to $218.7 million. Management claimed that this was because of several new projects ramping up this year compared to last year, and they also said that favorable weather conditions at the start of this year helped to boost revenue.

The other part of the company is the Materials segment, which focuses on aggregates, asphalt, and other similar offerings. Year over year, this segment saw revenue expand by 10.2% from $77.1 million to $84.9 million. Higher aggregates and asphalt volumes, as well as higher aggregates sales prices, helped to push sales higher. The firm also benefited from its acquisition of Dickerson & Bowen, Inc., an aggregate, asphalt, and highway construction company located in central and southern Mississippi. That particular purchase cost shareholders $125.5 million and was completed in August of last year. This added $5.6 million to the top line for the company for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. And that doesn't even include the $10.1 million of construction revenue it generated under the Construction segment.

Even though revenue for the business improved, profitability did worsen slightly. The firm went from a net loss of $31 million to a net loss of $33.7 million. This was primarily because of a surge in selling, general, and administrative costs from 13.09% of sales to 16.57%. The good news was that this was largely because of rising stock-based compensation, so it ultimately ended up being a non-cash expense. Actual adjusted net profits improved from negative $9.2 million to positive $0.2 million. While operating cash flow for the company contracted from $24.1 million to $3.6 million, the picture does look different if we adjust for changes in working capital. In this case, we would get a rise from $4.9 million to $31.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $14.1 million to $28.1 million.

All things considered, I would say that 2025 is looking to be a good year for the business. When it comes to the year as a whole, management anticipates revenue of between $4.2 billion and $4.4 billion. At the midpoint, that would represent an increase of 7.3% compared to the $4.01 billion the company reported last year. Management also expects the firm’s EBITDA margin to be between 11% and 12%. Hitting the midpoint here would mean EBITDA of $494.5 million, compared to the $401.7 million the business reported for 2024. Using the same year-over-year growth rate for the other profitability metrics would give us adjusted net profits of $263.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $388.3 million.

With these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. On a forward basis, I would say that things are looking pretty solid. But even if we use the historical results for 2024 instead of the estimates for this year, shares look to be on the cheap side of the spectrum relative to other similar enterprises. In the table below, you can see precisely what I mean. On a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, I found that only one of the five companies that I compared Granite Construction to ended up being cheaper than it is. And on a price to operating cash flow basis, our candidate was cheaper than three of the five competitors.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Granite Construction 18.7 12.7 10.9
MYR Group (MYRG) 83.8 18.3 22.6
Construction Partners (ROAD) 90.6 25.1 28.8
Primoris Services (PRIM) 21.2 7.3 10.5
Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) 27.0 13.5 14.4
Ameresco (AMRC) 14.9 11.7 12.0

The good news is that, as time goes on, Granite Construction has the opportunity to continue expanding. Organic growth was something that I talked about in my previous article, with that growth attributable to additional investments being made by the public and private sectors in construction activities in the markets in which the firm operates.

In addition to this, however, management is focused on other opportunities. For instance, it has made clear that it intends to continue pursuing acquisitions, while simultaneously making investments in things like automation and growing its core competencies. Geographic expansion is one form that revenue growth could take, both through organic means and by means of acquisition. For instance, through its purchase of Dickerson & Bowen, Inc., as well as its purchase of Lehman-Roberts and Memphis Stone & Gravel, the company has successfully expanded its physical footprint.

Moving forward, management expects organic growth alone to add revenue of between 6% and 8% annually. But when you add on top of this the prospect of potential acquisitions, we could see even greater upside. The scale that the business should achieve along the way should allow it to grow its bottom line as well. For 2027, for instance, the company is targeting an EBITDA margin of between 12% and 14%. It's also hoping to achieve an operating cash flow margin of between 9% and 11%. If we ignore the prospect of additional acquisitions, this would likely mean EBITDA of about $598.1 million and operating cash flow of about $430 million by 2027. Assuming that adjusted net profits rise at the same rate as operating cash flow, we would get a reading for it of about $291.8 million.

Even if Granite Construction continues to trade at the low multiples it is trading at as of this writing, this could mean attractive upside. In the chart below, you can see how much in aggregate upside shares should experience, as well as annualized upside, that they should experience, if the firm trades at the end of 2027 with the same multiples as we get when using the 2024 figures today. This upside of between 13.2% and 18.8%, on an annualized basis, would place the company above the 10% to 11% that the S&P 500 has averaged over time. And when you add on top of this the prospect of acquisition activities that could boost sales and profits even more, you can understand why I am bullish about the firm.

Even though I am bullish about Granite Construction, it is important to note that there are certain risks that investors need to be aware of. There are two in particular that come to my mind. The first relates to the fact that the vast majority of the business involves the public sector. The firm has what is called CAP, which stands for ‘Committed and Awarded Projects’. This can basically be equated to a backlog. At the end of the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, 80.6% of the firm’s CAP, or about $4.62 billion, involved the public sector. Right now, I think all of us would agree that we are facing a time of heightened political uncertainty. And at the end of the day, there is always some risk that changes in funding could negatively affect its ability to generate revenue from public projects.

Another risk relates to the nature of the firm's contracts and the role that tariffs might have moving forward. The company's contracts largely involved fixed unit price arrangements and fixed price arrangements. Fixed unit price arrangements are essentially those that involve a pre agreed price per unit of work for the company. An example here might be a certain dollar amount for each cubic yard of concrete that it lays. The beauty of this type of contract is that it pushes the risk of changes in quantity onto its customers. However, inflationary pressures, as well as other issues, can negatively affect the firm's profitability. At the end of its 2024 fiscal year, 59.1% of the companies' unearned revenue fell under the fixed unit price category.

Another 33.2% of its unearned revenue involved fixed price contracts. These are, according to management, priced on a lump sum basis. This means that the company accepts all of the risk, including those involving changes in the quantity of materials that it uses. With tariffs now in play, both types of contracts could see the company faced with pressures. But the good news here is that, in the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year, the firm mentioned that it had not seen any material changes to its pricing from the tariffs that have already been implemented. They very well could, but only time will tell what will transpire on that front. Certainly, this is something investors should be paying attention to.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I do believe that the upside for Granite Construction isn't over yet. The company continues to achieve attractive growth, and shares remain attractively priced compared to other similar firms and on an absolute basis. Even if the company doesn't achieve the kind of growth it is targeting, I don't think it would be a mistake to be cautiously optimistic. And if it does achieve its targets, then upside should be sufficient enough to justify a solid ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: AWF: Preserves Investor Capital While Delivering Monthly Income
data.description:
Overview

AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund (NYSE:AWF) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns by focusing on income generation through a portfolio of global fixed income securities. An income fund like AWF can be an efficient way to offset the market volatility experienced with traditional equities. The global exposure also means that investors have the opportunity to reduce their reliance on US indexes to fuel growth or income. The fund has an inception dating back to 1993, meaning we have more than three decades worth of operating history to reference. I wanted to cover AWF and discuss why I think it offers stability and resilience that would be attractive for retired investors who want to prioritize income and capital preservation.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that AWF's price has increased by about 5.3%. The fund has been able to regain some positive price action following the market decline caused by President Trump's tariff proposals back in March and April, which reinforces the efficiency of the fund's strategy. When including all distributions paid out, the total return jumps up to about 13.4% over the same period. The main appeal of holding AWF is the generous starting dividend yield of 7.2% and monthly payouts, which makes it enticing for investors that want to create an income stream with dividends.

AWF generates its earnings from different channels of income securities, so the fund has demonstrated a sensitivity to the interest rate environment. However, management has proven that the fund can maintain an attractive level of NAV resilience, which has preserved investor capital for several decades. Collecting a high rate of income doesn't mean that your invested capital needs to decay over time, and AWF is proof of that. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that AWF implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy & Risk

According to the fund overview, AWF has total assets of $971.9M that are spread across 1,241 different holdings. The fund is focused on income generation, so the portfolio is comprised of different bonds and high-yield debt securities. Non-investment grade Corporate bonds make up the largest bulk of assets at 79.17%. Therefore, most of AWF's ability to provide attractive total returns is dependent on how profitable and resilient these corporate bonds are. The emphasis on 'non-investment grade' borrowers means that AWF's risk profile can be elevated in this high interest rate environment. However, AWF offsets this risk by providing investors with global exposure. The breakdown by its top regions are as follows:

  • United States: 67.62%.
  • France: 3.49%.
  • United Kingdom: 3.04%.
  • Canada: 2.59%.
  • Brazil: 1.88%.
  • Mexico: 1.78%.
  • Italy: 1.33%.
  • Germany: 1.14%.
  • Spain: 1.13%.
  • Colombia: 1.09%.
  • India: 0.94%.

Non-investment-grade credit ratings indicate that a borrower may be more reliant on debt, have less consistent cash flows, or demonstrate more vulnerability to shifts in consumer spending. However, the increased risk profile also means that these securities typically offer investors a higher yield to offset.

Just for reference, borrowers with credit ratings below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade. The lower the grade, the higher the chance of defaults occurring, which would negatively impact AWF's earnings and NAV resilience. The breakdown by grouping is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 17.94%.
  • Non-Investment Grade (BB - CCC & Below): 78.1%.
  • Not Rated: 2.45%.

The problem with this is breakdown is that it becomes more risky during periods of elevated interest rates. For reference, the Federal Funds rate remains near its decade highs at the moment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher interest expense that borrowers must pay. While this can be beneficial for AWF since the fund may be able to collect a higher amount of interest income, it can also put strain on borrowers that are underperforming. A higher cost of debt can cause strain for certain borrowers and ultimately lead to defaults.

While AWF doesn't use traditional leverage in the sense of borrowing capital, it does use leverage through something labeled as 'investment operations'. This can be through certain derivatives or structured instruments, but the fund update doesn't specify these details. All that is stated is that AWF's leverage is equivalent to 18.61% of assets. Leverage can amplify the fund's losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

S&P Global estimates that defaults can increase to 1.6% by December 2025, in their base scenario. This assumes a slowing economy and rising prices that create headwinds for borrowers. However, the full impact of President Trump's tariff proposals are still to be determined, so there's a chance that defaults reach 3% by December 2025. Even if AWF doesn't directly experience any defaults, the outlook around the debt market could shift to a more negative sentiment and take valuations lower.

Valuation

Since AWF operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, the price now trades at a small discount to NAV of 2.45%. AWF has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.24% over the last three-year period, which can indicate that the price is a bit expensive right now. However, I don't necessarily think that the discount level is as important for long-term holders of AWF.

In order to get better clarity on the internal performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.75 per share, while net realized gains were much smaller at $0.06 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income has grown! This can be attributed to the higher interest rate environment, which has allowed AWF to collect a higher amount of income from its portfolio. While the performance here has improved, net investment income levels can easily decline in the future when interest rates are cut.

On the other hand, net realized gains are inconsistent over the last few years. This is because net realized gains rely on positive market conditions so that AWF is able to sell securities off at a higher value than they were purchased for. As an example, markets declined throughout 2022 and 2023 in reaction to the Fed aggressive hiking interest rates. As a result, AWF experienced net realized losses during this time period. Despite these shifts based on market conditions, AWF has demonstrated the ability to preserve its NAV over time.

  • 2021: $12.78 per share.
  • 2022: $12.02 per share.
  • 2023: $10.56 per share.
  • 2024: $11.21 per share.
  • 2025: $11.21 per share.

So, what does all of this data mean for investors? The most notable aspect of AWF's performance would be the ability to preserve investor capital over its lifetime. Since inception, AWF's price has only declined by about 3.3%. However, the total return including distributions now sits above 1,844% since inception! This long-term performance demonstrates that management has figured out a way to consistently generate income and provide distributions through different market cycles, recessions, global pandemics, and other one-off events.

I do believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a positive catalyst for growth. The price currently remains below its inception price, which could be an attractive point of entry. Looking over the fund's history, we can see that AWF is fully capable of providing capital appreciation when market conditions are positive. The only issue is that the Fed continues to leave interest rates unchanged because they want to monitor macroeconomic data for now. Unemployment is trending upwards, inflationary data still remains higher than anticipated, and the Fed is monitoring shifts in consumer spending levels.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0655 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.2%. Looking back over the last decade, AWF has been able to keep payouts consistent at this level since 2017. This level of income stability is a great match for retired investors that want to secure a source of reliable and consistent dividend income to fund lifestyle expenses.

Despite the fund not delivering any sort of base distribution raises, this level of consistency must be appreciated because it provides investors with the opportunity to create their own dividend growth. Just to demonstrate this, I ran a back test of an initial investment of $25,000 at the start of 2016. The graph below assumes that no additional capital was ever added after the initial $25k. However, it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into accumulating more shares of AWF. By simply holding and reinvesting distributions, an investor would have been able to see their dividend income grow.

2016 Dividend Income: $2,010

2024 Dividend Income: $3,425

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from AWF are primarily funded by net investment income. Therefore, distributions are typically classified as ordinary dividends, which have the least favorable tax consequences for investors. Based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital being invested, or your desired income level, a position in AWF may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate AWF as a buy due to the fund's resilience, ability to preserve its NAV, and consistent dividend history. I like the fund's diverse approach on a global scale, and I think the risk of defaults is limited. I believe that future interest rates can serve as a positive catalyst for growth as it would provide some relief to existing debt securities, as well as encourage more growth in markets. The fund still trades at a discount to NAV, and the dividend consistency allows long-term investors to create their own growth through reinvestment or consistently contributing new capital.

data.title: Healthpeak Offers An Attractive Dividend Yield With Room For Growth
data.description:
Healthpeak Properties (NYSE:DOC), now trading under the familiar ticker DOC following its acquisition of Physicians Realty Trust, looks a lot different than the company that once focused on nursing homes and hospitals. By combining Healthpeak’s life sciences and CCRC platforms with Physicians Realty’s massive medical office footprint, management created a 52 million square foot REIT that collects rent from surgeons, biochemists, and retirees. And within 12 months, the new entity internalized 19 million square feet of property management, saved more than $50 million in overhead costs, and increased its quarterly dividend.

These moves have not yet convinced investors. Now, the stock price has hovered around $18, even after a bond sale in February at just 102 basis points above Treasury bonds (the smallest spread ever for Healthpeak) and purchases on the open market by the chief executive and two directors. In other words, management is buying while the market sits back.

That mismatch is exactly why I like the stock. At the $18 price, investors get paid nearly 7% in monthly dividends, which is funded by steady rents for medical offices. The downside looks like getting paid to wait, and the upside looks like a 25-30% share price appreciation plus dividends along the way. That risk-reward is interesting and the reason I'm bullish here.

Lab Supply Grows But Healthpeak’s Balance Sheet Stays Solid

Venture capital for biotech startups dried up in 2023. Although Healthpeak’s labs remain nearly fully leased, a surge in newly completed space in Boston and San Francisco is now coming online, raising concerns that vacancy rates may soon begin to increase. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve kept policy interest rates above 5%, raising borrowing costs for every landlord. And because Healthpeak owns 11 million square feet of lab space and carries about $11 billion in debt, many investors worry that its cash flow and property values are vulnerable.

Quarterly figures suggest a softer landing. In the first quarter, adjusted FFO held steady at $0.46 per share, same-store NOI rose 7% and the net debt to EBITDA ratio ended at 5.2x. Management confirmed expectations for the full year and still has $2.8 billion in liquidity. None of that changed the story. The merger increased the number of shares outstanding, making per-share metrics appear flat, while investors have shifted their attention to pure senior housing REITs like Welltower that are recovering quickly from the pandemic.

Outpatient Clinics Deliver Steady Cash and Face Little Competition

More than half of Healthpeak’s rental income comes from outpatient medical buildings—typically low-rise clinics located on hospital campuses or in fast-growing areas. Rent increases average 3% to 4% annually, rent collection remains near 100%, and lease expirations typically account for less than 8% of annual rental income. Hospitals prefer them because they allow procedures to take place outside costly inpatient facilities without losing oversight. Doctors value the proximity to referral sources, and insurers appreciate the lower costs.

And new competition is scarce. A 20%-25% increase in construction costs and tighter bank credit have stalled projects that were little more than sketches in 2022. As a result, medical office completions for 2024 and 2025 combined barely match a normal year before COVID. Healthpeak thus benefits from both sides of the equation: reliable user demand and limited supply. The merger doubled Healthpeak's bargaining power with health systems. The company now leases space to almost every major nonprofit chain, and internal management allows Healthpeak to bring in revenue from services previously outsourced to third parties. In short, outpatient medical care may not be on the rise, but it rarely disappoints.

Long-Term Growth Drivers Stay Intact

The lab portfolio is drawing most of the attention. Occupancy is still around 98%, but rental rates have fallen, and concessions have been made to smaller tenants. The problem is timing: lab space planned during the genomics boom was completed just as borrowing costs surged, and biotech IPOs came to a standstill. Management anticipated the shift early and stopped new ground-up projects in 2021 to limit exposure. Nevertheless, about a million square feet of leases still expire through 2026, and some will be renewed.

Looking beyond, the case for real estate in coastal laboratories remains robust. The United States still dominates drug discovery, and both political parties favor domestic bioproduction after supply chain pain during COVID. Big pharma is facing a wave of patent expirations and is investing heavily in late-stage drug development to stay ahead. Meanwhile, Washington is debating extending exclusivity terms for small-molecule drugs, a change that would give venture capital investment a huge boost. As capital rises, Healthpeak's modern campuses in South San Francisco, Torrey Pines, and Cambridge should grab the first uptick, as they offer an authorized laboratory infrastructure that newcomers would take years to replicate. Investors who pay 10 times the expected FFO get a free option on that revival.

Demographic Tailwind And A Tight Supply

Senior housing was hit hard during COVID, but Healthpeak’s entrance-fee continuing-care campuses are already bouncing back: occupancy climbed from roughly 83% in late 2023 to 86% in Q1 2025, lifting same-store NOI by about 16%. Healthpeak owns 15 of them, mostly in affluent suburbs where the average owner-occupied home exceeds half a million dollars. And each new resident adds an extra-large profit because many costs are fixed.

In the United States, the 80-plus population is on track to grow almost 30% before 2030, yet developers are adding fewer senior-housing units than at any point in decades. For CCRCs, the barriers to entry are even higher, given regulations regarding startup costs and zoning hurdles. Healthpeak already posted 16% NOI growth same store in the first quarter. Even if that pace cools, management expects high single-digit increases to continue until 2026, when occupancy returns to pre-COVID peaks and rent increases are implemented.

Strong Balance Sheet With Mostly Fixed Debt

Healthpeak carries moderate debt, most of it locked in for years, so higher rates would barely dent its finances. Only $450 million matures before 2026; the average maturity is 5 years; more than 90% is fixed. February's 10-year note at 5.375% replaced cheaper but volatile commercial paper with a long-dated fixed-rate bond, trading cost for certainty. Each jump of 100 basis points in LIBOR or SOFR now raises annual interest rates by only 3 cents per share, an acceptable headwind.

Cash generation covered every promise. An adjusted FFO of $0.43 versus a quarterly dividend of just over $0.30 leaves a payout ratio of nearly 70% and free cash for share repurchases or small developments. During the first quarter, Healthpeak retired 1.1 million shares for $22 million at an average price of $19.45; between 1 April and 24 April, it bought another 3.9 million shares at $18.22, lifting YTD repurchases to 5 million shares and $94 million. With liquidity of nearly $3 billion, Healthpeak can pick out acquisitions, fund outpatient developments, or simply continue to shrink the float while peers tap equity at richer multiples.

Dividends And Valuation

At the current rate, the $1.22 annual dividend yields roughly 6.8%, the highest among large healthcare REITs. Competitor Ventas yields 3%; Welltower barely 2%. The coverage and lease term give it room to increase payouts once the labs' headwinds subside. Even flat dividends provide a nice total return combined with modest FFO increases due to synergy benefits and higher occupancy rates in CCRCs.

Expressed in valuation, Healthpeak is changing hands at about 10x times next year's FFO, with a growth expectation of about 5%. Ventas trades at around 18x times for an expected increase of 13%, and Welltower sits around 30x with growth estimates of 16%. Measured as “multiples of dollars per growth point,” that works out to about 2.0x for Healthpeak, 1.4x for Ventas, and 1.9x for Welltower. So on this ratio, Healthpeak is in between.

The appeal lies elsewhere: you get the highest cash yield in the group, the deepest discount to private market asset values, and a balance sheet that can withstand interest rate shocks. In my view, this trade-off (accepting a slightly higher multiple per point of growth in exchange for more income, more collateral, and less financial risk) is tilted toward Healthpeak.

Risks To Mention

There are risks to mention, such as biotech funding may take longer to recover, reducing lab rents and requiring write-offs; the Fed may remain restrictive, raising cap rates and squeezing any REIT multiple; a new health scare may scare away seniors, slowing the rebound of CCRCs. Yet, each comes with a cushion. Laboratory leases have an average term of 6 years, and many tenants have pharmaceutical balance sheet guarantees. Exposure to interest rates is usually hedged until late in the decade. CCRCs target affluent seniors who can still move even if the housing market slows.

Investment View

It’s rare to find a stock that yields nearly 7%, delivers mid-single-digit earnings growth, and still trades roughly 30% below the private-market value of its real estate. The discount persists because investors focus on today’s soft lab demand, overlooking steady demand from an aging population and the near-full occupancy of its hospital-linked medical offices. While bioscience headlines may remain dismal through 2025, the strength of ambulatory care and CCRC's momentum ensures that dividends remain secure and FFO remains flat or rising. If capital finally returns to biotech or if the Federal Reserve hints at easing, the stock's multiple should migrate to peers closer to 12x or 13x times FFO, implying a price in the twenties before asset sales or further buybacks.

That's why I give Healthpeak a buy rating. The market gives you a solid healthcare landlord at a nice price. Receive the monthly dividend, let time heal the lab market, and watch a forgotten REIT return to the spotlight.

data.title: Crocs: Rising Demand, Strong Profits, And A Discounted Stock Price
data.description:
The financial world is no stranger to misinformation and manipulation. A recent example is Builder.ai. The startup, once valued at $1.5 billion, was revealed to be far from the AI-driven company it claimed to be. Instead, its AI-app service was a team of 700 engineers based in India.

Doing your diligence to be sure you don’t get exploited by misinformation and manipulation has never been more critical. This applies to all kinds of investing, even dividend strategies, which most people consider to be safe and simple. The truth is that not all dividends are real. Some are false, paid out of cash balances or borrowings. Only diligent analysis can uncover the truth about whether a company can afford to keep paying its dividend.

Diligence not only helps expose potential pitfalls but also reveals strong investment opportunities. We’re specifically talking about businesses with solid fundamentals and stock prices that are undervalued. Our system is designed to identify exactly these types of stocks, which we feature as Long Ideas.

Humans have been wearing shoes for thousands of years. Today, the quality and types of shoes available for purchase are staggering, with thousands of brands competing for consumer dollars. With casual footwear gaining popularity, love them or hate them, this footwear company is poised to continue growing for the foreseeable future.

The best news for investors? This company is one of the most profitable in the retail footwear space, yet its stock is down year to date and trades at a steep discount. Crocs Inc. (NASDAQ:CROX) is this week’s Long Idea.

CROX Offers Favorable Risk/Reward Based On The Company’s:

  • position to benefit from the rising demand for casual fashion,
  • ability to take market share across its addressable markets,
  • steadily increasing Crocs brand sales,
  • best in class profitability, and
  • cheap stock valuation.

Consumers Need Shoes

One kind of footwear, specifically clogs, have been grabbing consumers’ attention and money. Long gone are the days of clogs being overlooked, as even high fashion/luxury brands such as Gucci and Balenciaga have introduced clogs in their collections in recent years.

As the innovator of clog-style shoes, Crocs is in a great position to take advantage of the growing footwear demand. Crocs defines its total addressable market (TAM) in three markets, all of which are forecasted to grow globally through 2030.

  • Casual footwear market: forecasted to grow 7.4% compounded annually through 2030,
  • Clogs market: forecasted to grow 11.4% compounded annually through 2030, and
  • Sandals market: forecasted to grow 6.2% compounded annually through 2033.

Figure 1: Projected Casual Footwear, Clogs, and Sandals Market Growth: 2024 – 2030

Sources: Value Market Research, IndustryARC, Grand View Research, and New Constructs

Taking Market Share In Growing Markets

From its IPO in 2006 to its resurgence in the late 2010s, Crocs has consistently increased it brand awareness and popularity across the globe. The company aims to expand in what the company calls “Tier 1 Markets,” which include The United States, West Europe, India, China, Japan, and South Korea. According to management, these regions present the best opportunities to take market share.

In 2024, Crocs reported market share in its targeted markets in three categories:

  • Growing markets: less than 1% share in China, France, and Japan.
  • Emerging markets: between 1% and 2% share in Germany and India.
  • Established markets: greater than 2% share in the U.K., the U.S., and South Korea.

When we look at the entire global footwear market, Crocs’ ability to take market share (as measured by revenue as a % of overall industry sales) in a highly fragmented market shines. Crocs has grown its share of the global footwear industry from 0.3% in 2018 to 1.0% in 2024. See Figure 2.

Figure 2: Crocs’ Global Market Share: 2018–2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Building A Larger Footprint

Crocs still generates most of its sales via its wholesale channel (selling to a retailer that sells to end users), though just barely. In 2024, wholesale sales represented 50.3% of revenue, down from 52% and 54.9% in 2023 and 2022, respectively.

Over that same time, the company has increased sales through its direct-to-consumer (DTC) channel, which includes both e-commerce and company owned retail stores. In 2022, DTC sales made up 45.1% of total revenue. In 2024, DTC made up 49.7% of total revenue.

Growing sales through its own retail channels allows the company to better control marketing, messaging, brand image, and even sell discontinued or discounted products through its outlet retail locations. To better serve customers, Crocs has grown its global store count (including both Crocs and HEYDUDE brand stores) from 367 in 2019 to 442 in 2024. See Figure 3.

In the first quarter of 2025, the DTC revenues for Crocs and HEYDUDE brands increased 1.1% and 8.3% YoY, respectively.

Figure 3: Crocs’ Global Store Count: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Maintaining Pricing Power

In 2024, the Crocs brand accounted for 80% of revenue, while the HEYDUDE brand accounted for 20% of revenue.

Often, a business can grow market share by lowering prices to maximize quantity of sales. Crocs takes a different approach and has routinely taken market share by growing unit sales while its average selling price (ASP) rises.

On a yearly basis, Crocs brand unit sales almost doubled from 67 million in 2019 to 127 million in 2024, while its ASP increased from $18 to $26 over the same time. See Figure 4.

Both increasing unit sales and ASP drive Crocs brand’s revenue up from $1.2 billion in 2019 to $3.3 billion in 2024.

Figure 4: Crocs Brand Unit Sales and Average Selling Price: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

The Lagging Elephant in the Room

While the Crocs brand has achieved excellent sales growth, the same cannot be said for HEYDUDE, the popular footwear brand Crocs acquired in 2022.

In 2024, both unit sales and revenue of the HEYDUDE brand declined year-over-year (YoY). However, the brand remains nearly a billion-dollar segment (based on revenues), and signs of a turnaround are present.

As with the Crocs brand above, management has not resorted to price cuts simply to drive unit sales. Instead, HEYDUDE’s ASP grew YoY in 2024 and increased for the seventh consecutive quarter in 1Q25. It’s clear that management values protecting margins, rather than chasing unit sales (more details later in report).

Despite the sales decline in 2024, HEYDUDE remains popular across e-commerce platforms. For instance, the brand was ranked as a top 3 brand on TikTok Shop in December 2024 and had its first-ever TikTok Shop Super Brand Day in February 2025, where it ranked as the no. 1 footwear brand across the platform.

The number of TikTok shoppers in the U.S. grew an estimated 34% YoY in 2024, and the number of TikTok shoppers is estimated to grow 6% compounded annually through 2027. By leveraging a growing presence across social media, along with bringing in new “brand ambassadors”, Crocs is positioning HEYDUDE for future growth opportunities.

Improving Fundamentals

Despite the HEYDUDE challenge, Crocs is growing top- and bottom-line.

The company has grown revenue by 14% and net operating profit after-tax (NOPAT) by 15% compounded annually since 2006 (earliest data available).

More recently, the company improved its NOPAT margin from 4% in 2014 to 21% in the TTM, while invested capital turns fell from 1.3 to 1.0 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins are enough to offset falling invested capital turns, though, and drive return on invested capital (ROIC) from 5% in 2014 to 22% in the TTM.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings grew 41% compounded annually from $23 million in 2014 to $777 million in the TTM.

Figure 5: Crocs’ Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Best-In-Class Profitability

Crocs not only has more global market share than peers such as Birkenstock (BIRK) and Steve Madden (SHOO), 0.5% and 0.6% market share in 2024, respectively, but it is also one of the most profitable companies in the casual footwear industry.

Crocs has the highest NOPAT margin and second highest ROIC among publicly traded peers, which include Deckers Outdoor (DECK), Nike (NKE), Birkenstock (BIRK), Steve Madden (SHOO), VF Corporation (VFC), and more. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: Crocs’ Profitability Vs. Peers: Trailing 12 Months (TTM)

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Repurchase Yield Could Reach 4% Or More

Crocs has not paid and does not plan to pay dividends on its common stock.

However, the company does return capital to shareholders through share repurchases.

Crocs has repurchased $2.1 billion (37% of market cap) shares since 2019. In February 2025, Crocs’ Board of Directors approved a $1 billion increase to the company’s share repurchase authorization. As of March 31, 2025, the company has $1.3 billion remaining under this authorization.

If the company repurchases shares at the same rate as 2024, when it repurchased $552 million in shares, it would repurchase an additional $492 million worth of shares in 2025 (in addition to the $61 million repurchased in 1Q25). $492 million in share repurchases represents 8.6% of the company’s current market cap.

If instead the company repurchases shares on par with its 1Q25 rate, it would repurchase $243 million worth of shares over the next 12 months, which represents 4.2% of the company’s current market cap.

Crocs paused share repurchases while it completed its acquisition of HEYDUDE, but outside of that year, management has consistently repurchased shares as the company’s stock has gotten cheaper. Per Figure 7, share repurchases have risen as CROX’s price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio has fallen.

Figure 7: Crocs’ Share Repurchases and PEBV Ratio: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Cash Flow Picking Up Post Acquisition

Since 2019, Crocs has generated a cumulative $3.0 billion in free cash flow (FCF) excluding acquisitions, which equals 39% of its enterprise value.

When we include the acquisition of HEYDUDE in 2022, Crocs’ cumulative FCF is $309 million over the same time.

Perhaps most impressively, Crocs has rapidly improved FCF in the years since the acquisition. In 2023 and 2024, Crocs generated $1.8 billion in FCF. Crocs generated an additional $39 million in FCF in 1Q25.

Aggressively Paying Down Debt

Not only has Crocs used its strong free cash flow to repurchase shares, it is also paying down debt taken on for the acquisition of HEYDUDE.

Per Figure 8, Crocs’ total debt has declined from $2.8 billion in 2022 to $2.0 billion in 1Q25. We like companies that choose to return capital to shareholders and pay down debt, instead of spending it on exorbitant executive bonuses or more acquisitions.

Figure 8: Crocs’ Total Debt: 2022 – 1Q25

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Tariffs Could Potentially Hurt

Crocs management withdrew its full year 2025 guidance due to the uncertain impact of tariffs. However, management provided investors with an expense framework when they reported 1Q25 earnings. Specifically, management outlined two potential scenarios:

  1. “If we assume 10% incremental tariffs on all sourcing destinations into the US, this would translate to a cost of approximately $45 million on an annualized cash basis.”
  2. “If the incremental 145% tariff on China remains in place along with the 10% on all other sourcing destinations, this would imply a cost of approximately $130 million on an annualized cash basis based on our current sourcing mix.”

To offset tariff costs, management noted in the 1Q25 earnings call that they have already identified $50 million in potential savings throughout 2025.

Additionally, the company is focused on maintaining margins, rather than chasing sales volume, no matter what happens with tariffs.

From the 1Q25 earnings call, CEO Andrew Rees noted:

“I would rather increase prices, protect margin and have adequate room in the P&L to invest in marketing and consumer communication to drive consumers to our brand than take margin reductions and have no latitude to communicate effectively with consumers.”

Protecting margins at the potential expense of sales supports a strong brand image and reputation.

Though tariffs could add costs to Crocs’ business, the potential impact is already more than priced into the company’s stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Profits Will Permanently Fall By 20%

At its current price, Crocs’ price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio is 0.8. This ratio means the market expects the company’s NOPAT to permanently decline 20% from TTM levels. This expectation seems overly pessimistic, considering Crocs has grown NOPAT by 46% compounded annually over the last five years and 34% compounded annually over the last decade.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to analyze expectations for different stock price scenarios for CROX.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 15% (well below TTM NOPAT margin of 21%) through 2034, and
  • revenue grows at 2.5% compounded annually through 2034 (equal to one-third of the forecasted casual footwear market CAGR of 7.4%) then

the stock is worth $102/share today – equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, Crocs’ NOPAT would fall 1% compounded annually from 2025 to 2034.

For reference, if we add $130 million (equal to the higher tariff costs provided by management) to Crocs’ operating expenses, the company’s TTM NOPAT margin would fall to 18.7% from 21.4% over the TTM.

In other words, Crocs’ current share price implies the company’s margins will fall even further than management expects and not recover for the next decade.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Tariffs Cut Margins

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 18.7% (equal to implied margin in the worst tariff scenario outlined by management) through 2034,
  • revenue grows at consensus estimates in 2025 (1%), 2026 (3%), and 2027 (1%), and
  • revenue grows 2.5% each year thereafter through 2034 (equal to one-third of the forecasted casual footwear market CAGR of 7.4%), then

the stock would be worth $125/share today – a 23%+ upside to the current price. In this scenario, Crocs’ NOPAT would grow just 1% compounded annually through 2034. Should the company’s NOPAT grow more in line with historical levels, the stock has even more upside.

Figure 9 compares Crocs’ implied future NOPAT in these scenarios to its historical NOPAT.

Figure 9: Crocs’ Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Sustainable Competitive Advantages Will Drive Shareholder Value Creation

Here’s a summary of why we think the moat around Crocs’ business will enable it to continue to generate higher NOPAT than the current market valuation implies:

  • consistently taking market share in a growing market,
  • well-positioned in the casual shoes market around the world, and
  • expanding point of sale and store footprint across the globe

What Noise Traders Miss With Crocs

These days, fewer investors focus on finding quality capital allocators with shareholder-aligned corporate governance. Due to the proliferation of noise traders, the focus is on short-term technical trading trends while more reliable fundamental research is overlooked. Here’s a quick summary of what noise traders are missing:

  • growing demand for comfortable and casual footwear,
  • superior profitability compared to peers – both margins and ROIC, and
  • valuation implies profits will permanently decline by 20%.

Tariff Certainty Could Send Shares Higher

The on-again, off-again tariffs have wreaked havoc on management teams’ ability to reliably forecast sales and profits for 2025 and beyond. Should the market receive any certainty on tariffs, especially if they’re lower than the worst-feared 145%, it could send many retail stocks, including CROX, higher.

Exec Comp Could Be Improved

Crocs’ executives receive both annual incentives and long-term equity awards. The annual incentives are tied to adjusted earnings before interest and tax (EBIT), adjusted free cash flow, brand strategic initiative performance, and corporate responsibility and sustainability progress.

Long-term equity awards are tied to adjusted 3-year cumulative revenue and adjusted EBITDA operating margin.

We would prefer the company tie executive compensation to ROIC. Doing so ensures that executives’ interests are more aligned with shareholders’ interests, as there is a strong correlation between improving ROIC and increasing shareholder value. Improving ROIC requires attention to all areas of the business, so including it in an executive compensation plan would incentivize executives to improve the entire business, not just one sales goal or earnings number.

Despite using revenue and EBIT goals in its executive pay, Crocs’ management has grown economic earnings, the true cash flows of the business, 52% compounded annually over the last five years, from $53 million in 2019 to $472 million in the TTM ended 1Q25.

Insider Trading And Short Interest Trends

Over the past 12 months, insiders have purchased 326,529 shares and sold 151,063 shares for a net effect of 175,466 shares bought. These sales represent <1% of shares outstanding.

There are currently 6.0 million shares sold short, which equates to 11% of shares outstanding and just under three days to cover.

Attractive Funds That Hold CROX

The following funds receive an Attractive-or-better rating and allocated significantly to CROX:

  1. Catalyst/Lyons Tactical Allocation Fund (CLTIX) – 3.4% allocation and Attractive rating.
  2. Acquirers Fund (ZIG) – 3.3% allocation and Very Attractive rating.
  3. Wall Street EWM Evercore Equity Fund (EWMCX) – 2.6% allocation and Attractive rating.
  4. Invesco S&P MidCap 400 GARP ETF (GRPM) – 3.3% allocation and Very Attractive rating.
  5. Alpha Architect U.S. Quantitative Value ETF (QVAL) – 2.0% allocation and Very Attractive rating.

data.title: VNM And Nike: Vietnam Trade Deal Good, But Not Great, News
data.description:
President Trump announced a trade deal with Vietnam on 2nd July, as reported here by The New York Times, just a week before the tariff deadline. The deal removes the threat of 45% tariffs but still imposes tariffs of 20% on imports from Vietnam.
